planting.note: Direct sow at a support after frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep beside a tall support and train the climbing vines upward. This Italian heirloom, also called Marvel of Venice, bears wide, flat golden-yellow romano pods. Pick the tender pods young for the best flavor and continued yield.

planting.note: Sow at a support once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at the base of a trellis and let the vigorous vines climb. This Spanish heirloom produces very long, broad, flat green pods that stay tender at large size. Harvest the meaty pods while still smooth for the best eating.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and thin the bushes a few inches apart. This old commercial heirloom bears round, meaty, truly stringless green pods. Pick the pods young and keep the plants harvested for a longer season.

planting.note: Sow as soon as the ground can be worked in early spring for one of the earliest pea harvests.

directions: Plant seeds about one inch deep and one to two inches apart directly in cool, workable soil. This early smooth-seeded variety germinates in chilly conditions, so a short brushwood or trellis keeps the vines upright. Pick the plump, round pods once the peas inside have filled out for shelling.

planting.note: A very hardy dwarf pea suited to autumn or late-winter sowing in mild regions.

directions: Set seeds one inch deep in a double row, spacing them roughly two inches apart. This compact, cold-tolerant type rarely needs much support but appreciates a low twiggy frame. Harvest the short, well-filled pods while the peas are still tender and sweet.

planting.note: An early French petit pois grown for very small, sweet peas picked young.

directions: Sow one inch deep in cool spring soil and provide light netting for the slender vines. This heirloom is prized for tiny, tender peas, so pick the pods slightly before they fully swell. Frequent early harvests keep the plants producing over several weeks.

planting.note: A dwarf petit pois that produces heavy clusters of small, sweet peas.

directions: Plant the seeds an inch deep in a broad band and thin lightly if crowded. The short bushy plants carry pods in pairs, making picking quick and heavy. Gather the pods while the peas remain small for the sweetest, most tender flavor.

planting.note: Hardy enough for autumn or overwinter sowing in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow one inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil, spacing rows to allow easy picking. This early, cold-tolerant variety can be started in late autumn under cloches for a spring crop. Harvest the rounded pods when they feel full but before the peas turn starchy.

planting.note: A compact wrinkle-seeded pea bearing large, well-filled dark green pods.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep in cool soil and give the short vines a modest trellis. The blocky, deep green pods swell quickly and hold their sweetness on the plant. Shell the peas soon after picking, since sugars fade fast once harvested.

planting.note: An old English dwarf shelling pea valued for heavy early yields.

directions: Sow an inch deep in double rows during cool weather and support with short pea sticks. This heritage variety stays compact yet loads its stems with straight, well-filled pods. Pick regularly once the pods plump up to encourage a continued set.

planting.note: A miniature leafless pea ideal for containers and small beds.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and close together so the tendrils intertwine for self-support. This tiny variety needs no staking and thrives in pots on a sunny patio. Harvest the small pods early, or pinch the leafless shoots for tender salad tendrils.

planting.note: A semi-leafless type whose abundant tendrils let plants support one another.

directions: Plant an inch deep in a dense block so the heavy tendrils knit together and stand without staking. The reduced foliage improves airflow and keeps pods clean and easy to spot. Shell the plump pods promptly, and enjoy the extra tendrils as an edible garnish.

planting.note: Bred to hold its high sugar content longer after picking than most shelling peas.

directions: Sow one inch deep in cool, moist soil and give the vines a short net or twiggy support. This variety keeps its sweetness well, which makes it forgiving if picking is delayed a day or two. Gather the dark green pods once they feel firmly filled with peas.

planting.note: An early, disease-resistant dwarf shelling pea popular for spring planting.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in fertile soil as early as the ground allows. The compact plants resist common pea diseases and set large, dark pods on short vines. Pick the pods once they are well rounded for the best yield of sweet peas.

planting.note: A maincrop semi-leafless pea grown for long, well-filled pods.

directions: Sow one inch deep in a broad band so the many tendrils lock together for support. This maincrop variety produces long, slightly curved pods each holding many peas. Harvest when the pods are full but still bright green for peak sweetness.

planting.note: An old English field pea grown for brown maple-colored dried peas.

directions: Sow seeds one to two inches deep and provide a tall trellis for the vigorous vines. Grown chiefly as a drying pea, the plants are left until the pods and seeds mature fully. Harvest when the pods turn papery and the maple-brown peas rattle inside, then dry them further before storing.

planting.note: A dwarf snap pea offering early edible-pod harvests on short vines.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in cool soil and offer a short trellis for the compact vines. Pick the thick, rounded pods while crisp and sweet, before the peas swell too large. Frequent harvesting through the cool weeks keeps new pods forming.

planting.note: A yellow-podded snap pea with sweet, translucent golden pods.

directions: Sow one inch deep at the base of a tall trellis, since these vines climb energetically. The pods ripen to a glowing gold and stay tender and sweet for eating whole. Pick them when plump and still crisp, and let a few mature fully for saving golden seed.

common_name: Royal Snap II Purple Snap Pea

planting.note: A purple-podded snap pea that is easy to spot against green foliage.

directions: Plant an inch deep beside a sturdy tall trellis and let the bicolor-flowered vines climb. The deep purple pods contrast with the leaves, making picking quick and easy. Harvest while the pods are crisp; they cook up green and sweet when snapped young.

planting.note: An early, compact snow pea producing tender flat pods on short vines.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in cool spring soil and give the short vines light netting. Pick the flat pods while young and stringless, before the peas begin to bulge. Regular picking every couple of days keeps this early variety cropping heavily.

planting.note: A stringless snow pea bearing clusters of flat pods over a long season.

directions: Plant an inch deep in cool soil and support the vines with a low trellis or net. Pods form in pairs and clusters, so a single picking yields a good handful. Harvest the flat, stringless pods while thin and crisp for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: A French heirloom snow pea named for its large, curved ram's-horn pods.

directions: Sow one inch deep at the foot of a tall support for the vigorous climbing vines. The pods grow unusually long and curved, resembling a ram's horn when mature. Pick them young and flat for stir-fries, before the peas inside develop.

planting.note: A cold-hardy small-seeded fava good for overwintering and cover cropping.

directions: Sow seeds one to two inches deep in cool soil, either in fall in mild regions or very early spring. This hardy, small-seeded variety survives hard frosts and also serves as a nitrogen-fixing cover crop. Harvest young pods for whole eating or let the beans mature for shelling and drying.

planting.note: A dwarf fava ideal for windy sites and small gardens needing no staking.

directions: Plant seeds two inches deep, spacing them for the bushy dwarf habit that rarely needs support. The compact plants are well suited to exposed or windy gardens and containers. Pick the short pods young for whole cooking or leave them to fill for shelling.

planting.note: A fast, early fava favored for spring sowing and quick pods.

directions: Sow two inches deep in early spring for a rapid crop of long, well-filled pods. The sturdy plants benefit from a stake or string as the heavy pods develop. Harvest while the beans are green and tender, pinching the tops to deter blackfly.

planting.note: A heirloom fava prized for its deep crimson-red blossoms.

directions: Sow two inches deep in cool soil and support the stems as they grow tall. Beyond the edible beans, the striking crimson flowers make it worthy of an ornamental border. Harvest the pods while young and tender, and enjoy the blooms drawing bees to the garden.

planting.note: A reliable heirloom longpod fava suited to autumn or spring sowing.

directions: Plant seeds two inches deep, either in late autumn under cover or in early spring. This dependable heritage longpod produces heavy crops of well-filled pods. Support the tall plants and pick the pods before the beans grow tough.

planting.note: A longpod fava whose beans keep a green color when cooked or frozen.

directions: Sow two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and stake the tall stems. The long pods hold many green-seeded beans that keep their color after cooking or freezing. Harvest while the beans are young and their scars still pale green.

planting.note: A fava producing rose-pink seeds unusual among broad beans.

directions: Sow two inches deep in cool soil and support the plants as pods form. The shelled beans are an attractive rose-pink, adding color to the plate. Pick pods young for tender beans, or let a few mature to save the distinctive pink seed.

planting.note: An heirloom fava bearing crimson-red seeds and bronze-tinged flowers.

directions: Plant seeds two inches deep in cool weather and stake the tall stems for support. This heirloom yields striking crimson beans that fade toward gold when cooked. Harvest the pods young for the best texture and richest color.

planting.note: A very compact dwarf fava well suited to pots and close spacing.

directions: Sow seeds two inches deep in blocks; the tiny plants support one another without staking. Its small stature makes it ideal for containers, raised beds, and tight spaces. Pick the short pods young and whole, or shell the small beans when filled.

planting.note: An adaptable fava that can be sown in fall or spring for large green beans.

directions: Sow two inches deep in autumn under cover or in early spring once soil is workable. The vigorous plants produce upright pods packed with large, mild-flavored green beans. Stake as needed and harvest before the beans become mealy.

planting.note: An exhibition longpod fava bearing very long pods with many beans.

directions: Plant seeds two inches deep in rich soil and give the tall stems firm support. Each long pod can hold eight or more green beans, making it a favorite for showing. Harvest the pods while the beans are young and their eyes remain pale.

planting.note: A dependable crowder pea for hot, humid summers.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil, spacing them a few inches apart in rows. This bushy crowder sets silvery-green pods that hold tightly crowded seeds. Shell the peas at the fresh green stage, or leave the pods to dry for winter use.

planting.note: A large-seeded crowder valued for high yields of big peas.

directions: Sow one inch deep in warm soil after frost, giving each plant room to bush out. The long pods hold large, crowded reddish-brown peas that are excellent shelled fresh. Harvest at the green-shell stage for eating or let the pods dry fully for storage.

planting.note: A cream pea whose pods shell easily, as if unzipped.

directions: Sow one inch deep in warm soil, spacing plants a few inches apart in the row. The plump pods have a seam that pulls open cleanly, making shelling fast and easy. Pick at the green-shell stage for tender cream-colored peas with a mild flavor.

planting.note: A compact bush blackeye pea with concentrated pod set for easy picking.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil once nights stay mild. The tidy bush plants hold their pods above the foliage for quick, clean harvest. Shell the classic black-eyed peas at the green stage or dry them on the vine.

planting.note: An early cream pea with a compact habit and quick harvest.

directions: Sow one inch deep in warm soil after frost for one of the earliest cream pea crops. The small bush plants set pods in a concentrated flush that ripens together. Shell the small cream peas fresh, or let a portion dry for later cooking.

planting.note: A small-seeded cream pea prized for its delicate flavor and clear pot liquor.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and space the compact plants a few inches apart. The tiny cream-white peas cook up mild and tender, without darkening the broth. Harvest at the green-shell stage or leave the pods to dry for storing.

planting.note: A small semi-vining Southern cream pea popular in the Gulf states.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and allow room for the semi-vining plants to spread. The slender pods are packed with small tan-white peas of fine texture. Pick at the green-shell stage for the classic flavor prized in Southern cooking.

planting.note: Direct sow once soil is reliably warm, as this variety needs a long, hot season.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in rich, well-drained ground and allow the vigorous vines wide spacing. The deeply lobed fruit ripens from green to a rich mahogany-tan as it matures. Harvest when fully colored and the stem is dry, then cure in a warm spot to deepen the sweet, musky flesh.

planting.note: Direct sow once nights stay warm and frost has passed.

directions: Sow two or three seeds per hill an inch deep and thin to the best plant. These small, deep-orange pumpkins carry sweet, dry flesh well suited to baking. Harvest when uniformly colored and the rind resists a fingernail, then cure before storage.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, warm bed.

directions: Set seed an inch deep in warm soil on the semi-compact, space-saving vines. Each plant yields several palm-sized, round orange pumpkins ideal for decorating. Pick when fully orange with a firm rind and a corked stem.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after spring frosts end.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and let the running vines climb a trellis to save space. Each vine sets many small, deeply ribbed orange pumpkins that keep well for months. Harvest when the fruit is fully colored and the skin is hard.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes into warm soil.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and keep vines evenly watered through fruit set. This AAS winner bears small, flattened pumpkins with sturdy handles and semi-hulless edible seeds. Cut when the rind is fully orange and firm, leaving the stem intact.

planting.note: Sow once the soil warms after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep in fertile ground and water consistently. These small, round pie pumpkins offer sweet, fine-grained flesh with little waste. Harvest when the rind is deep orange and resists a thumbnail.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep on the semi-bush plants that suit smaller gardens. The near-black acorn fruit has thick, nearly stringless orange flesh. Cut when the rind is hard and the ground spot has turned deep orange.

common_name: Cream of the Crop Acorn Squash

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep on the compact, semi-bush vines. This AAS winner bears striking cream-white acorn fruit with pale, nutty flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and the skin has fully whitened.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the vines room to run. This heirloom acorn-type bears creamy-ivory fruit with sweet, smooth flesh reminiscent of sweet potato. Harvest when the rind is fully colored and hard, then cure for best flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow once the soil is warm after frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep on the semi-bush, powdery-mildew-tolerant plants. This improved acorn holds its sweet, dry flesh well in storage. Cut the dark-green fruit when the ground spot is orange and the rind resists a fingernail.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warmed ground.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. This variety is noted for an especially hard rind that gives excellent winter storage life. Harvest the ribbed green fruit when fully mature and the skin cannot be dented.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Set seed an inch deep and give the vines steady moisture and full sun. The teardrop-shaped, orange-red fruit carries smooth, chestnut-flavored flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and glossy, then cure before storage.

planting.note: Direct sow into warmed soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and allow the large vines ample room. This Pacific Northwest heirloom bears slate-gray, drum-shaped fruit with thick, sweet orange flesh that stores for many months. Harvest when the rind is fully hardened and cure in a warm place.

planting.note: Direct sow once soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Set seed an inch deep and let the vines run in full sun. This distinctive heirloom forms three-lobed, blue-gray fruit that keeps for a year or more. Harvest when the rind is fully hard and cure to firm the sweet flesh.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and provide steady water as fruit forms. This popular hybrid bears steel-blue, flattened fruit with deep-orange, nutty flesh that stores for months. Harvest when the rind is fully hard and cure in a warm spot.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil; suits small gardens and containers.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep on these compact, bush-habit plants that need little space. Each plant yields several small butternut-type fruit with sweet, deep-orange flesh. Harvest when the rind turns solid tan and the stem corks.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep on these semi-vining, space-efficient plants. This AAS-winning buttercup-type bears dark-green, turban-shaped fruit with sweet, dry orange flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and the ground spot has colored.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and give the vines room to run. This classic heirloom buttercup bears dark-green, turban-capped fruit with sweet, dry, almost sweet-potato-like flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and the ground spot has turned buff.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and water steadily as fruit forms. This AAS-winning buttercup produces blocky, dark-green fruit with exceptionally sweet, fine-textured orange flesh. Harvest when the rind is fully hard and cure before storing.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the vigorous vines wide spacing. This variety produces large, dark-orange carving pumpkins with sturdy dark handles. Harvest when the fruit is fully orange and the rind is hard, cutting the stem cleanly.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and water steadily as fruit sizes up. This early-maturing hybrid sets uniform, round orange pumpkins with strong handles, useful in short-season areas. Harvest when fully colored and the rind resists a thumbnail.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and provide consistent moisture on the vigorous vines. This hybrid produces tall, uniform orange pumpkins with dark, durable handles and good disease tolerance. Harvest when fully colored and the rind is hard.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and let the vines run in full sun. This hybrid yields uniform, deep-orange carving pumpkins with strong handles and reliable production. Harvest when fully colored and the rind cannot be dented.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and keep vines evenly watered. This popular white pumpkin has ghostly pale skin over bright-orange flesh good for carving and baking. Harvest when the skin is fully white and the rind is firm, shading fruit from direct sun to keep the color pure.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and give the large vines wide spacing. This variety produces big, blocky white pumpkins prized for painting and display. Harvest when the skin is fully white and the rind is hard, handling the heavy fruit by supporting the base rather than the stem.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and water steadily through the season. This variety bears smooth, medium-sized pure-white pumpkins popular for autumn decorating. Harvest when the skin is fully white and the rind is firm.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and let the vines run. This colorful variety sets small, flattened fruit mottled in orange, blue-gray, and green, both decorative and edible. Harvest when the rind is hard and the colors are fully developed.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and grow for the seeds rather than the flesh. The small orange-and-green-striped fruit holds dark, hull-less seeds excellent for roasting. Harvest when the rind hardens, then scoop and dry the naked green seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and grow chiefly for the seed crop. This Austrian oilseed type produces large, dark-green striped fruit filled with hull-less seeds used for roasting and pressing into oil. Harvest when the rind hardens, then extract and dry the naked seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and let the vines run in full sun. This Japanese heirloom ripens from blackish-green to a warm tan-buff, with nutty, chestnut-flavored flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and cure to deepen the color and flavor.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile soil for this long-season type.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the sprawling vines generous room. This heirloom forms long, cylindrical, pink-skinned fruit with sweet, fine-grained orange flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and cure before slicing into portions for storage.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost for this long-season variety.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and allow the vigorous vines ample space. This heirloom bears long, blue-gray banana-shaped fruit with dense, sweet orange flesh that stores well. Harvest when the rind is fully hard and cure in a warm spot.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the long vines wide spacing. This Appalachian heirloom produces long, pink-orange fruit with a blue tip and exceptionally sweet, smooth flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and cure before baking or storage.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and let the vines run in full sun. This 19th-century heirloom bears teardrop-shaped, slate-blue fruit with dense, sweet, fine-grained orange flesh that keeps for many months. Harvest when the rind is hard and cure before storing.

planting.note: Sow after frost; needs a long, hot season to sweeten.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the rampant vines maximum room and heat. This variety forms long, curved, tan fruit with a small seed cavity and very sweet orange flesh. Harvest when fully tan and cure for several weeks to develop its melon-sweet character.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and give the vines a long, warm season. This Italian heirloom produces heavily wrinkled, violin-shaped tan fruit with sweet, deep-orange flesh. Harvest when the rind is fully colored and hard, then cure before storage.

planting.note: Sow after frost into rich, warm soil for this long-season heirloom.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and give the powerful vines wide spacing. This Italian heirloom forms very long, cylindrical dark-green fruit that ripens to tan, with sweet orange flesh concentrated in the seedless neck. Harvest when fully colored and cure before slicing into sections.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and allow the vigorous vines ample room. This heirloom forms long, curved-neck tan fruit with dense, sweet flesh and a small seed cavity in the bulb. Harvest when the rind is fully tan and hard, then cure before storage.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep and keep vines evenly watered through fruit set. This hybrid butternut-type bears frosted silvery-tan fruit with sweet, deep-orange flesh and good storage life. Harvest when the rind is fully colored and hard, then cure before storing.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and give the vines room to run. This Southwestern heirloom bears blocky, pale-gray fruit with sweet, fine-textured orange flesh well suited to dry climates. Harvest when the rind is fully hard and cure before storage.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil a week or more after the last frost.

directions: Set seeds about an inch deep where they will get full sun all day. Bred for larger fruit, it produces meaty zucchini that hold up well to slicing and grilling. Let the squash reach eight to ten inches, then cut before the seeds toughen.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start early under cover for cool-weather setting.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil, or start indoors for the earliest fruit. Because it sets parthenocarpically without pollination, it produces well in cool or wet spells when bees are scarce and even under row cover. Pick the dark green fruit at six to seven inches for the best texture.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when the soil is warm.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This bright yellow scallop-edged squash is best gathered small and tender for the finest texture. Pick the flying-saucer fruit at two to three inches across and keep harvesting to sustain the plants.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after frost danger has ended.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, compost-enriched soil in full sun. This light green scallop squash is tender and productive on a compact bush. Harvest the disk-shaped fruit young at two to three inches for the sweetest, most delicate flesh.

planting.note: Sow after frost once the ground has warmed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, fertile soil with full sun. The bright yellow scallop fruit show a distinctive green star at the blossom end and hold quality well. Gather at two to three inches for tender eating and steady production.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant an inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. This dark green scallop squash sets heavily on a tidy open bush that is easy to reach into. Cut the fruit small at two to three inches and pick often to keep new ones coming.

planting.note: Sow after frost when the soil is dependably warm.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, fertile soil in full sun. The bicolor green-and-yellow scallop fruit are decorative as well as tasty when picked small. Harvest at two to three inches across for tender flesh, or leave a few to grow larger for stuffing.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm ground.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in rich, sunny soil. This green scallop-type squash blends pattypan shape with zucchini flavor on a compact plant. Pick the round, fluted fruit at two to three inches for the most tender eating.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil in full sun. The nearly spineless stems and open plant make picking this smooth yellow straightneck fast and comfortable. Harvest at six to seven inches while the skin is bright and tender.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich, sunny soil once warmth is steady. This vigorous hybrid carries glossy medium-green fruit on an open, easy-to-pick bush. Harvest at six to eight inches and remove overgrown squash to keep production high.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, compost-rich soil. This vigorous virus-tolerant hybrid holds medium-green, faintly flecked fruit on an open plant. Harvest at six to eight inches and pick daily during the peak of the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when the soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a warm, sunny bed. An older award-winning hybrid, it sets uniform glossy green fruit steadily through summer. Harvest at six to eight inches and keep cutting so the bush keeps flowering.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, sun-warmed soil. This classic hybrid produces smooth dark green cylindrical fruit early and heavily. Cut the zucchini at six to eight inches while young and tender for the best kitchen use.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Plant an inch deep in rich, sunny ground. This grey-green speckled zucchini is early and productive on a compact bush. Harvest the mottled fruit at six to eight inches and pick frequently to sustain the plant.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This bright golden zucchini-type squash holds its color and stays tender on an open bush. Cut the fruit at six to eight inches and harvest daily for a steady supply.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, sunny soil. This Middle Eastern cousa-type squash bears tapered pale green fruit prized for their nutty flavor and firm flesh. Harvest at three to five inches for the sweetest, most tender eating.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, sun-warmed soil. This pale green cousa-type zucchini is early and tender with a mild, sweet flavor. Harvest at four to six inches while the skin is still soft and glossy.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm ground.

directions: Plant an inch deep in rich, sunny soil. This heirloom Italian zucchini bears pale, almost silvery green fruit on a productive open bush. Cut at six to seven inches for tender flesh and pick often to keep it setting.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, sunny soil once it is warm. This classic Milanese heirloom carries dark green, lightly ribbed fruit on a compact dwarf bush. Harvest at six to eight inches while young for firm, flavorful zucchini.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, compost-rich soil. This Italian heirloom bears ribbed fruit striped light and dark green with firm, flavorful flesh. Harvest at six to eight inches and cut regularly to keep new fruit forming.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, sunny ground. This Ligurian heirloom produces pale grey-green fruit and abundant blossoms good for stuffing. Pick the fruit at six to eight inches, or gather the flowers early in the day for cooking.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, sunny ground. This Italian round zucchini forms tender green globes that are excellent hollowed and stuffed. Harvest at two to four inches across while young, and keep cutting to keep the bush productive.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm ground.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, fertile soil in full sun. This compact hybrid bears bright yellow pear-shaped fruit on a tidy, early bush. Harvest at three to four inches for tender flesh, or let them grow a bit larger for stuffing.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich, sunny soil. Bred to produce an abundance of large male blossoms, this squash is grown mainly for its edible flowers. Pick the open flowers in the morning for frying or stuffing, and gather any young fruit at six inches.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, sunny ground. This hybrid forms bright golden-yellow oval fruit on a compact, productive bush. Harvest the small egg-shaped squash at two to three inches for tender flesh, picking often to keep them coming.

planting.note: Sow after frost in a spot with room for the semi-compact vines to run.

directions: This All-America Selections hybrid has shorter, semi-bush vines that fit smaller gardens while still producing full-size slicers. Its strong disease resistance rewards steady watering and a little support. Harvest the eight-inch fruit young and continuously to extend the long bearing season.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost danger is fully past.

directions: A vigorous all-female hybrid bearing slender, thin-skinned burpless fruit prized for its sweet, non-bitter flavor. Trellising keeps the long cucumbers straight and easy to find. Pick at eight to ten inches and harvest often for the tenderest fruit.

planting.note: Sow directly after the soil warms in late spring.

directions: A long burpless hybrid that yields mild, crisp fruit up to a foot long with excellent disease tolerance. Grow on a fence or trellis so the fruit hangs straight and stays clean. Cut the cucumbers young and keep the vines picked to maintain a heavy, extended harvest.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil and give the vines a trellis to climb.

directions: This ribbed Japanese-type hybrid produces tender, sweet, easy-to-digest fruit that grows longest when trained upward. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed lightly to sustain the heavy yield. Harvest at eight to ten inches for the crispest, mildest cucumbers.

planting.note: Sow at the base of a trellis after the soil warms.

directions: An old vigorous heirloom with strong tendrils that readily scramble up netting or fences, saving ground space. The slim green fruit is crisp for slicing or pickling and is easy to spot on the vertical vines. Keep well watered and pick young to encourage continued flowering.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil; this Indian heirloom loves heat.

directions: An Indian heirloom whose fruit ripens from pale white through golden yellow to russet brown, staying crisp and sweet at any stage. It tolerates heat well and produces heavily even in trying summers. Eat them white for the mildest flavor or let them turn brown for a more russeted skin.

planting.note: Sow directly once soil warms; performs well in humid climates.

directions: A widely grown early hybrid slicer valued for strong disease tolerance and steady output in tough conditions. Space plants a foot apart and support the vines to keep fruit clean. Harvest the straight seven- to eight-inch cucumbers young and often.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil for a reliable pickling harvest.

directions: A parthenocarpic pickling hybrid that sets fruit without pollinators, making it a good pick for tunnels and row cover. Keep the vines evenly watered and support them for cleaner, straighter cucumbers. Harvest small and often for crisp gherkins and dill pickles.

planting.note: Sow after frost; sets fruit even without bees under cover.

directions: A parthenocarpic cornichon-type hybrid that yields thin, spineless fruit ideal for French-style pickles. Because it needs no pollination, it thrives under row cover that also blocks beetles. Pick very young at two to four inches for crisp gherkins, checking daily during the flush.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil for a compact pickling crop.

directions: A productive gherkin-type pickler bearing tender, thin-skinned fruit on manageable vines. Sow in rows and keep the plants consistently moist to prevent bitterness and misshapen fruit. Harvest small and frequently to keep new pickling cucumbers forming.

planting.note: Sow after frost for a steady supply of tiny French pickles.

directions: A traditional French cornichon that bears prolific slim, spiny fruit meant to be picked tiny. Grow on the ground or a low trellis and keep the bed moist for tender skins. Harvest daily at one to three inches to pack into classic vinegar cornichons before they enlarge.

planting.note: Direct sow once the soil warms in late spring.

directions: A French heirloom pickler with slender black-spined fruit suited to both tiny cornichons and larger dill pickles. Keep vines evenly watered so the cucumbers stay firm and straight. Pick small for gherkins or let a few grow to four or five inches for standard pickles, harvesting often.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: A French heirloom cornichon prized for prolific, very spiny little fruit with excellent pickling flavor. Grow along the ground or a short trellis and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest tiny and daily during the peak to stay ahead of the fast-sizing fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a dependable early heirloom.

directions: A 1920s American heirloom that produces smooth, crisp green fruit good for slicing and early pickling. Sow in warm soil and thin to a foot apart or trellis to keep fruit clean. Pick at six to eight inches and harvest regularly for a long picking window.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; well suited to short, cool summers.

directions: One of the oldest American heirlooms, this early variety sets small green fruit in pairs and matures quickly for northern gardens. Its short season makes it a good choice where summers are brief. Pick the three- to five-inch cucumbers young for slicing or pickling and harvest often.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil for a disease-tolerant pickling crop.

directions: A pickling variety bred for broad disease resistance, including good tolerance to downy and powdery mildew. It sets blocky white-spined fruit well suited to humid regions where disease pressure is high. Keep evenly watered and harvest small and frequently for quality pickles.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil for a concentrated pickling set.

directions: A productive gynoecious pickling hybrid that flowers heavily and sets a heavy, uniform crop for processing. Include a few pollinator plants nearby to ensure full fruit set. Keep the bed moist and pick small and often for the crispest pickles.

planting.note: Sow after frost for a heavy cornichon-to-pickle crop.

directions: An All-America Selections pickling hybrid that yields smooth, slightly spined fruit good for cornichons when young and dills when larger. It carries solid disease resistance and produces abundantly. Keep the soil moist and harvest frequently across the whole size range.

planting.note: Sow after frost and give the long fruit a trellis to hang from.

directions: A slim, dark, thin-skinned slicer that produces long burpless fruit best grown vertically for straight shape. Keep vines evenly watered and feed lightly during heavy bearing. Harvest at eight to twelve inches while the skin is smooth and glossy.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a versatile dual-purpose heirloom.

directions: A German heirloom whose warty green fruit is good young for pickling and slightly larger for slicing. It performs well in cooler climates and produces reliably over a long season. Pick small for pickles or up to six inches for fresh use, harvesting regularly.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a hardy German pickling heirloom.

directions: A prolific German heirloom whose name refers to the grape-like clusters of fruit it sets along the vine. It is grown mainly for pickling but the young fruit is also good fresh. Keep the plants well watered and pick the little cucumbers often to sustain the heavy clusters.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil and trellis the vigorous vines.

directions: A Japanese burpless variety bearing long, slender, mild fruit with tender skin and few seeds. It grows straightest on a trellis and tolerates disease well. Harvest at ten to twelve inches for sweet, non-bitter slicing cucumbers.

planting.note: Sow after frost at the base of a sturdy trellis.

directions: A ribbed, spiny Chinese-type cucumber that produces long, sweet, burpless fruit prized for its crunch. Grow it upward so the fruit hangs straight and stays free of curl. Pick at ten to fourteen inches while the skin is still bright green and the spines rub off easily.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; the original of the Marketmore line.

directions: The earliest release in the Marketmore breeding line, this open-pollinated slicer set the standard for smooth, dark, uniform fruit. Space plants a foot apart and keep evenly watered for straight cucumbers. Harvest at seven to eight inches and pick regularly for continued production.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; sets fruit well under row cover.

directions: A parthenocarpic pickling hybrid that produces glossy, spineless fruit without needing pollination, so it excels under protective cover. Keep the vines evenly moist for firm, blocky cucumbers. Harvest small at two to four inches for gherkins and check daily during peak set.

planting.note: Sow after frost; well suited to greenhouse and tunnel growing.

directions: A parthenocarpic Beit alpha hybrid that bears smooth, thin-skinned mini cucumbers with no bitterness and no need for pollination. Train the vines on strings in a tunnel or greenhouse and prune side shoots for airflow. Harvest at five to six inches for sweet, crisp fruit.

planting.note: Sow after frost; ideal for containers and protected culture.

directions: A parthenocarpic mini cucumber that sets sweet, seedless snack-size fruit without pollination, making it a favorite for containers and greenhouses. Train the vines upward and keep them evenly watered and fed. Pick the fruit at four to six inches for the crispest, thin-skinned snacking.

planting.note: Sow after frost and trellis for straight, long fruit.

directions: A Japanese long hybrid producing slender, ribbed, dark green fruit with tender skin and mild sweet flavor. It needs a trellis to grow the fruit straight and reaches over a foot long. Harvest at ten to twelve inches and pick often to keep the vines bearing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm, fertile soil.

directions: A dependable slicing variety that yields uniform, straight fruit about nine inches long with a crisp, mild interior. Give the vines room to run or a trellis to climb and water steadily. Harvest at seven to nine inches while the skin is dark and glossy.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a vigorous Himalayan heirloom that likes room.

directions: A large Himalayan heirloom whose big fruit ripens to a rusty reddish-brown with a netted skin, first noted by growers in the Sikkim region. The vigorous vines need plenty of space and appreciate a strong trellis. Eat young and green for slicing or let them ripen brown for a fuller flavor and easy seed saving.

planting.note: Sow after frost; an Australian heirloom that tolerates heat.

directions: An Australian heirloom bearing rounded, apple-shaped green fruit with crisp, mild, non-bitter flesh. The productive vines handle heat and dry spells better than many types. Pick the fruit while still green and about the size of a small apple for the best texture.

planting.note: Sow after frost; compact enough for containers and small beds.

directions: A true dwarf-vine variety whose compact habit suits patio pots and small gardens without a trellis. Despite the short vines it produces full-size crisp slicing fruit. Keep containers well watered and harvest at six to eight inches to keep the plant setting more.

planting.note: A space-saving bush form; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this compact, short-vined selection fits containers and small beds. Keep watering steady through fruit set on the tidy plants. The round dark green icebox melons are ready when the belly spot yellows and the tendril dries.

planting.note: A long-season Western heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and give the vines room to sprawl. This heirloom bears blocky green melons with sweet, fine-grained red flesh. Harvest when the ground spot turns yellow and the tendril nearest the stem dries.

planting.note: A thin-rinded heirloom best sown into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this heirloom is famed for exceptionally sweet flesh and a thin rind that ships poorly but tastes wonderful fresh. Give the vines room and steady moisture through fruiting. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the nearest tendril dries.

planting.note: Sow into fully warmed soil for these long striped melons.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and thin to vigorous plants with wide spacing. This popular variety yields long, oblong striped melons with firm sweet red flesh that hold well after picking. Harvest when the belly spot yellows and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: A long-season hybrid; sow into warm soil after all frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and space the vines generously. This widely grown hybrid produces large oblong striped melons with crisp sweet red flesh and good field-holding ability. Pick when the ground spot yellows and the tendril by the stem dries.

planting.note: An early icebox hybrid; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil for this compact, early hybrid bearing small oval striped melons. Keep vines evenly watered through fruiting on space-friendly plants. The personal-sized melons are ripe when the belly spot yellows and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: An early small-fruited type; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this early variety yields small round melons with sweet yellow flesh. Keep watering steady while fruit sizes up on the modest vines. Harvest when the ground spot turns cream and the stem tendril withers.

planting.note: An early yellow-fleshed type; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this early open-pollinated variety ripens quickly even in cooler gardens. The small oval melons carry sweet yellow flesh in a pale-striped rind. Pick when the belly spot yellows and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: A large long-season heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give the vigorous vines wide spacing. This heirloom produces large oblong melons with bold green stripes and sweet, crisp red flesh. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the tendril nearest the stem dries.

planting.note: A round Southern heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and space the vines widely for full-sized fruit. This heirloom bears large round dark-green melons with tender sweet red flesh. Pick when the belly spot yellows and the nearest tendril dries.

planting.note: A striped heirloom; sow into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and thin to sturdy plants with plenty of running room. This heirloom yields round to oval light-green striped melons with sweet, crisp red flesh. Harvest when the ground spot turns yellow and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: A tough-rinded shipping heirloom; sow into warm soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and give the vigorous vines wide spacing. This old shipping heirloom produces long dark-green melons with a tough rind and sweet red flesh that travel well. Pick when the belly spot yellows and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: A drought-tolerant type; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this variety tolerates heat and dry conditions and resists sunburn on its pale rind. The round to oval melons hold sweet yellow-orange flesh. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the nearest tendril dries.

planting.note: A giant long-season type; sow into warm soil for maximum size.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in deeply enriched warm soil and give each plant a huge footprint. This is the classic giant-competition melon, capable of well over one hundred pounds with heavy feeding and abundant water. To grow record fruit, thin to one melon per vine and harvest when the ground spot yellows and the stem tendril dries.

planting.note: A classic netted heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and thin to strong plants. This famous Colorado heirloom produces heavily netted, round green-fleshed melons with rich aroma. Harvest at full slip, when the fruit parts from the stem with gentle pressure and the blossom end is fragrant.

planting.note: An Israeli heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this Israeli heirloom bears small round melons with green striping and very sweet, aromatic pale-green flesh. Keep moisture even through fruit set, then reduce as they ripen. Pick when the rind yellows between the green ribs and the fruit smells fragrant.

planting.note: A small Japanese melon; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this Japanese heirloom bears small round golden melons eaten skin and all like an apple. Keep watering steady through fruiting on compact vines. Pick when the rind turns deep yellow and the flesh is crisp and sweet.

planting.note: A French heirloom cantaloupe; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this old French monastery melon has a nearly black-green ribbed rind that turns orange as it ripens. The deep orange flesh is richly perfumed and sweet. Harvest when the rind shifts toward orange and the fruit slips or softens at the stem.

planting.note: A large Southern heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this heirloom produces large, deeply ribbed oblong melons with fragrant sweet orange flesh. Give the vines room and steady moisture through fruiting. The thin-rinded fruit is ripe when it perfumes strongly and slips from the vine easily.

planting.note: A firm-rinded heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this heirloom bears large heavily netted melons with a firm rind that stores and ships better than most. The thick orange flesh is sweet and aromatic. Harvest at full slip when the netting is coarse and the blossom end smells fragrant.

planting.note: A fragrant Middle Eastern heirloom; sow into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this old Middle Eastern melon carries lightly netted oval fruit with sweet, pineapple-scented white to pale-green flesh. Keep moisture even until the melons begin to color. Harvest when the rind turns golden and the fruit smells intensely fragrant.

planting.note: A flavorful early hybrid; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this early hybrid is valued for rich flavor and good disease tolerance. The netted round melons hold sweet, aromatic salmon-orange flesh. Pick at full slip when the fruit lets go with a gentle tug and the blossom end is fragrant.

planting.note: A small fragrant heirloom; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this old fragrance melon was once carried in pockets for its powerful sweet scent rather than eaten. The small orange fruit is mottled with maroon stripes and only marginally palatable. Harvest when the rind turns orange and perfumes the air strongly.

planting.note: A small culinary melon for preserves; sow into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this heirloom bears small peach-sized orange-yellow fruit with bland firm flesh best cooked into pickles, preserves, or pies. Keep watering steady on the productive vines. Harvest when the fruit turns golden and detaches easily from the stem.

planting.note: An East Asian melon; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this East Asian favorite produces small oblong yellow melons with white ribs and crisp, mildly sweet white flesh eaten skin, seeds, and all. Keep vines evenly watered through fruit set. Harvest when the rind turns bright yellow and the fruit smells lightly sweet.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Set seed pieces four inches deep and hill generously, as this variety produces tubers readily near the surface. The round red-skinned tubers tolerate heavy soils better than many types and are good all-purpose potatoes. Dig new potatoes early or leave the crop to size up until the vines die back.

planting.note: Plant near the last frost in loose, fertile soil.

directions: Plant the yellow seed tubers four inches deep and hill soil around the vigorous plants. This German variety has smooth yellow skin and firm, waxy flesh that resists breaking apart when boiled. Harvest new potatoes after flowering or leave the crop to mature for storage.

planting.note: Plant near the last frost date in fertile soil.

directions: Plant the small knobby tubers four inches deep and hill soil gently around the stems. This French gourmet fingerling has a distinctive nutty, chestnut-like flavor and waxy texture. Harvest after the foliage dies back and enjoy the tubers steamed or in salads.

planting.note: Plant early, as soon as the soil can be worked.

directions: Plant the deep-red seed tubers four inches deep and hill soil around the stems as they emerge. This fast early variety produces smooth, dark-skinned tubers ideal for boiling and new potatoes. Begin digging tender new potatoes soon after flowering.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in fertile soil.

directions: Plant the yellow-fleshed seed tubers four inches deep and hill as the plants grow. This European favorite has a slightly sweet, buttery flavor and creamy texture good for mashing. Harvest after the tops die down and store the tubers in a cool, dark spot.

planting.note: Plant early in spring once soil is workable.

directions: Set the red-skinned tubers four inches deep and hill soil around the emerging stems. This early variety has red skin over golden flesh and a smooth, buttery flavor best enjoyed fresh. Dig new potatoes shortly after flowering for the best texture.

planting.note: Plant after frost in loose, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the deep-red seed tubers four inches deep and hill soil as the plants develop. Sangre holds its bright color well and produces smooth tubers good for boiling and roasting. Harvest after the foliage dies back and store the crop in cool darkness.

planting.note: Plant early once the ground can be worked.

directions: Set the seed pieces four inches deep and hill the rows as the plants grow. Superior is an early, scab-resistant white potato with a dry, mealy texture good for boiling and baking. Harvest new potatoes soon after flowering or leave the crop to size up.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant cured seed pieces four inches deep and hill soil through the season. This high-yielding variety has round, netted-skin tubers with white flesh popular for chips and crisps. Harvest after the vines die back and cure the tubers before storing.

planting.note: Plant after frost for a mid-season harvest.

directions: Set the seed pieces four inches deep and hill the rows as the plants grow. Shepody produces long, oblong tubers with white flesh that is a classic French-fry potato. Water steadily through tuber formation and harvest after the vines die back.

planting.note: Plant early in spring for a fast first crop.

directions: Plant the seed pieces four inches deep and hill soil around the plants as they grow. Onaway is one of the earliest varieties, producing round white tubers well suited to short-season gardens. Begin digging new potatoes shortly after the plants flower.

planting.note: Give this long-season salad potato an early start after frost.

directions: Plant the knobby pink-skinned tubers five inches deep and hill soil around the tall haulm. This heritage salad fingerling has firm, waxy yellow flesh with a distinctive nutty flavor. Leave the crop until autumn, then lift and store the curved tubers in a cool place.

planting.note: Chit and plant out after the last hard frost.

directions: Plant the yellow-skinned tubers five inches deep and hill soil as the plants grow. Vivaldi is marketed as a lower-carbohydrate potato with smooth skin and creamy, moist flesh. Harvest once the plants flower for a versatile boiling and baking potato.

planting.note: Plant after frost for a long-season floury maincrop.

directions: Set the russet-skinned tubers five inches deep and keep the rows hilled through the long season. This Scottish heirloom has very floury flesh prized for chips and roasting. Harvest after the haulm dies back and cure the tubers, which store well over winter.

planting.note: Chit and plant out after the last frost for early kidney-shaped potatoes.

directions: Plant the seed tubers four inches deep and hill soil as the plants develop. Grown on Jersey as the Jersey Royal, this early has waxy, kidney-shaped tubers with a delicate flavor. Dig the new potatoes soon after flowering and eat them fresh for the best taste.

planting.note: Plant after frost for an early maincrop harvest.

directions: Plant the seed tubers five inches deep and hill the rows as the plants grow. Pentland Dell produces long oval tubers with floury white flesh good for roasting and chips. Harvest after the haulm dies back and store the tubers in a cool, dark place.

planting.note: Plant after frost in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the red-skinned tubers five inches deep and hill soil as the plants develop. Romano is a popular maincrop with creamy flesh that stays firm and is good for boiling and baking. Harvest after the vines die back and store the well-keeping tubers cool and dark.

planting.note: Chit and plant out once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant the tubers five inches deep and hill soil around the plants as they grow. Kestrel has striking white skin with purple splashes around the eyes and smooth, pale flesh. A popular show and kitchen variety, it is ready for lifting once the plants flower.

planting.note: Plant after frost for a long-season heirloom purple crop.

directions: Set the deep-violet tubers four inches deep and hill soil around the plants. This old French variety has knobby dark skin and dense purple flesh with a nutty, chestnut flavor. Lift the tubers after the vines die back and cook to preserve their striking color.

planting.note: Chit and plant out after the last frost for a salad crop.

directions: Plant the small yellow tubers four inches deep and hill soil as the plants grow. This classic French salad potato has firm, waxy yellow flesh with a rich buttery flavor. Harvest after flowering and use the tubers boiled or in salads while fresh.

planting.note: Plant after frost in loose, fertile soil.

directions: Set the red-skinned tubers four inches deep and hill soil around the plants. Roseval is a French salad potato with rosy skin and firm, slightly yellow waxy flesh. Harvest after flowering and enjoy the tubers steamed, boiled, or roasted whole.

planting.note: Chit and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Plant the pale-yellow tubers five inches deep and hill soil as the plants grow. Estima is a reliable, heavy-cropping second early with light-yellow flesh good for boiling and baking. Harvest once the plants flower and store any surplus in a cool place.

planting.note: Plant chitted tubers after the last frost.

directions: Set the yellow-skinned tubers five inches deep and hill soil around the growing plants. Wilja is a dependable, high-yielding second early with dry, pale-yellow flesh that holds its shape. Harvest once the plants flower for a versatile all-purpose potato.

planting.note: Sow as soon as the soil can be worked into a fine, stone-free seedbed.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in loose, deeply dug soil and keep the surface evenly damp until sprouts appear. Thin young plants to a couple of inches apart so the smooth, nearly coreless Nantes-type roots can size up. Pull when roots reach six to seven inches and a sweet, tender flavor develops.

planting.note: Give this large storage type a long season by sowing in late spring for a fall crop.

directions: Plant seed shallowly in deep, well-loosened ground that lets the heavy tapered roots grow straight. Thin generously since mature roots run large and need room to bulk up. Harvest the broad-shouldered orange roots in autumn and store them cool for winter use.

scientific_name: Daucus carota subsp. sativus 'Jaune du Doubs'

planting.note: This old French field carrot needs a full season, so sow from spring into early summer.

directions: Sow the fine seed a quarter inch deep and firm the soil lightly for good contact. Thin seedlings so the large pale-yellow roots have space to lengthen and swell. Lift the mild, faintly sweet roots for the table or as fodder once they reach full size.

common_name: White Belgian Carrot (Blanche a Collet Vert)
scientific_name: Daucus carota subsp. sativus 'Blanche a Collet Vert'

planting.note: A vigorous heirloom that grows partly above ground, so sow in deep loose soil.

directions: Drill seed a quarter inch deep in rich, deeply worked soil and keep moist through germination. Thin plants well apart since roots grow long and thick with green-shouldered white flesh. Harvest the large mild roots for cooking or livestock feed before hard freezes arrive.

planting.note: Sow late spring into early summer to mature this long-season storage carrot for fall.

directions: Plant seed shallowly in fertile, well-drained beds and keep the row evenly watered while slow to sprout. Thin the seedlings so the conical deep-orange roots develop their full aromatic sweetness. Harvest in fall and store cool, where this open-pollinated variety keeps its rich flavor for months.

planting.note: Sow in spring for summer roots or midsummer for a fall harvest.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in loose soil and thin the seedlings to prevent crowding. Keep beds weed free and evenly moist so the bright yellow roots grow smooth and straight. Pull once roots reach a usable size, prized for a sweet flavor that holds up to cooking.

planting.note: A dependable Berlicum-type hybrid suited to storage; sow spring through midsummer.

directions: Drill seed shallowly in deep, stone-free soil and firm gently. Thin the stand so the strong cylindrical roots can fill out with blunt tips. Harvest the smooth uniform roots for fresh use or lift them in fall for reliable winter storage.

planting.note: This adaptable hybrid tolerates heavier ground; sow spring and again in summer.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and keep the seedbed damp during the slow germination. Thin plants to a couple inches apart for well-formed tapered roots. Harvest the vigorous, high-yielding roots as they reach mature size in cooler weather.

planting.note: A refined Nantes hybrid that holds well in the ground into fall.

directions: Plant seed shallowly in loose, fertile soil and thin seedlings early to avoid twisting. Water steadily to grow smooth, blunt, deep-orange roots with fine sweet flesh. Pull as needed once sized, or leave in the row where roots keep their quality for weeks.

planting.note: Vigorous Nantes-type hybrid; sow through the season for fresh and storage roots.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in deeply worked soil and keep evenly moist. Thin the seedlings so the long cylindrical roots grow uniform and crack resistant. Harvest the sweet, sturdy roots in fall; they store well thanks to strong tops that ease pulling.

planting.note: Sow when soil warms slightly in spring and repeat in late summer.

directions: Plant seed half an inch deep and thin the seedling clusters to give roots room. Maintain even moisture so the smooth, deep-red globes size up without cracking. Harvest young for baby beets or let them reach full size for slicing and roasting.

planting.note: A white heirloom beet; sow spring and late summer for tender roots.

directions: Plant seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings so the pure-white globes can round out. Keep the soil evenly moist to prevent the roots from turning woody. Harvest young for the sweetest flavor, and cook the crumpled leaves like chard.

planting.note: Bolt tolerant, so it can be sown a little earlier and later than most beets.

directions: Plant seed half an inch deep and thin the clusters to give each root space. Keep soil moist to grow smooth, round, deep-red beets with sweet flesh. Harvest at two to three inches, and use the glossy tops as cooking greens.

planting.note: A widely grown hybrid for fresh use and storage; sow spring through midsummer.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings early to prevent misshapen roots. Give steady water so the round red globes stay smooth and free of zoning. Harvest at bunching size or grow larger for storage, where the roots keep well into winter.

planting.note: A quick baby beet ideal for close spacing and successive sowings.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin lightly, since this variety tolerates tight spacing. Keep soil moist for fast, even growth of the small round roots. Harvest while still small and tender for baby beets and pickling.
